elements.title: NAFTA is Gone — Meet USMCA
elements.content: You might hear the word NAFTA — the North American Free Trade Agreement between Canada, the US, and Mexico from 1994. Here is what you need to know: NAFTA does not exist anymore. In 2020, it was replaced by the USMCA (United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement) — an updated version. Then in 2025, Trump started threatening tariffs on Canada and Mexico anyway, even though USMCA was supposed to prevent that. This is why trade agreements are complicated — they only work if countries actually follow them.

elements.content: Blood diamonds funded a civil war that killed over 50,000 people in Sierra Leone. The diamonds ended up in jewelry stores around the world. The people who bought them had no idea.

elements.title: Pre-Film Context — Read This First
elements.content: Where: Sierra Leone, West Africa. When: 1991-2002. What happened: A brutal civil war funded largely by the illegal diamond trade. Rebel groups forced people — including children — to mine diamonds at gunpoint. Those diamonds were sold internationally, financing weapons and violence. The globalization connection: The global demand for diamonds — especially in wealthy countries like Canada, the US, and the UK — created the market that made conflict diamonds profitable. Without buyers, there would be no incentive. This is what economists call demand-driven exploitation.

elements.title: What Happened Next
elements.content: This video was made in 2012. The Northern Gateway pipeline was ultimately rejected by the federal government in 2016, partly due to Indigenous opposition. However, the Trans Mountain pipeline expansion — a different route — was approved and completed in 2024 after years of legal battles and protests. First Nations along the routes were divided — some opposed on environmental and rights grounds, some negotiated equity stakes worth hundreds of millions of dollars. The debate about who owns the land, who bears the risk, and who benefits from Alberta's oil is still very much alive.

elements.content: In 2023, companies started using AI to replace the outsourced workers in call centres in India and the Philippines. Those workers had already replaced workers in Canada and the US. Now AI is coming for the jobs of everyone, everywhere. Where does it stop?

elements.title: The Three Waves of Outsourcing
elements.content:
Wave 1 — Manufacturing (1970s–1990s): Factories moved from Canada, the US, and Europe to countries with cheaper labour — China, Bangladesh, Mexico, Vietnam. Workers in rich countries lost manufacturing jobs. Workers in poor countries gained them, often in difficult conditions.

Wave 2 — Knowledge Jobs (1990s–2010s): Technology made it possible to outsource thinking jobs too — call centres, IT support, software development, accounting. A computer programmer in India could be hired by Microsoft for $17,000 a year instead of $98,000 in the US. The knowledge economy was supposed to protect Western workers — it didn't.

Wave 3 — AI (2020s–now): AI is replacing the outsourced workers. Call centres in the Philippines that replaced Canadian workers are now being replaced by chatbots. Entry-level jobs for new graduates in coding, customer service, writing, and data are disappearing. The question now isn't which country gets the jobs — it's whether humans get the jobs at all.

elements.title: A New Question — Welcome to RI4
elements.content:
You just finished Related Issue 3: Does globalization contribute to sustainable prosperity for all people?

Now we move to Related Issue 4: Should I, as a citizen, respond to globalization?

This is a different kind of question. RI3 was about understanding globalization — what it does, who it helps, who it hurts. RI4 is personal. It's asking what YOUR responsibility is. As a Canadian. As a young person. As someone who buys things, votes eventually, and lives in a globalized world.

The first thing we need to figure out is: what does a good life actually look like? And does globalization help or hurt that?

sessions.title: Session 1 — Quality of Life: What Does It Actually Mean?

elements.content: Canada is one of the wealthiest countries on Earth. We have healthcare, clean water, free schools, and enough food. By almost every measure, we should be thriving. So why are young Canadians getting less happy every year?

elements.instruction: Type each definition.

elements.title: Two Ways to Measure a Good Life
elements.content:
Standard of Living measures the stuff — income, housing, access to goods, GDP per person. It's mostly numbers. Canada scores very high.

Quality of Life measures the experience — happiness, health, freedom, connection, safety, meaning. It's harder to measure because it's partly subjective. Canada's score has been falling for a decade.

The Human Development Index (HDI) tries to combine both — it looks at income, life expectancy, and education. Norway consistently ranks #1. Canada usually ranks in the top 20.

The gap between our standard of living (very high) and our quality of life (declining) is one of the most interesting puzzles in social science right now — and globalization is part of the answer.

elements.title: The Two Faces of Youth and Globalization
elements.content:
Globalization has opened doors for youth:
- Access to global information and education through the internet
- International youth movements (climate activism, human rights) connected through social media
- Economic growth in developing regions has reduced child poverty in many countries
- Global organizations like UNICEF advocate for children's rights worldwide

Globalization has closed doors for youth:
- The digital divide leaves hundreds of millions of children without internet access
- Child labour still drives supply chains for cocoa, cobalt, cotton, and garments
- Youth unemployment in developing regions is often higher than adult unemployment
- Conflict — sometimes fuelled by resource extraction — displaces millions of children
- Young Canadians are among the unhappiest youth in the world despite material wealth

elements.title: Globalization and Women: Two Stories
elements.content:
Story 1 — The cost side:
80% of garment workers globally are women. They work in Bangladesh, Cambodia, India, and Ethiopia for wages that often fall below poverty lines. Many cannot form unions, take sick days, or leave if conditions are dangerous. The Rana Plaza disaster in Bangladesh — where over 1,100 garment workers (mostly women) died when a factory collapsed in 2013 — showed the world what the hidden cost of cheap fashion looks like. Consumer demand in wealthy countries drives this system.

Story 2 — The opportunity side:
Globalization has also created new economic pathways for women. In countries like Rwanda, Kenya, and India, women have used mobile banking and microloans to start businesses that generate income independent of male family members. International organizations like UN Women and UNICEF have used global platforms to advocate for girls' education and women's rights. The Global Gender Gap Report tracks progress on economic participation, education, health, and political empowerment every year.

Both stories are true. Globalization exploits women AND creates pathways for women. The question is who controls which story.

elements.title: Transnational Communities
elements.content:
When people migrate, they don't just leave — they stay connected. They send money home (remittances), maintain cultural traditions, raise bilingual children, and often influence politics in both their home and new countries.

The World Bank reports that international remittances grew by 650% between 2000 and 2022 — from $128 billion to $831 billion annually. In many developing countries, remittances from citizens abroad are larger than all foreign aid and foreign investment combined.

Globalization made this possible. Cheap international calls, money transfer apps, and social media let people live in two places at once — what scholars call transnational communities.

Alberta is full of them — Filipino nurses sending money home to Manila, Ukrainian families split across borders, newcomers from India and Nigeria who maintain deep ties to their home countries while building lives here.

elements.title: GMOs, Agribusiness and the Green Revolution
elements.content:
The Green Revolution of the 1960s-70s used science and technology to dramatically increase crop yields worldwide, especially in Asia and Latin America. It saved hundreds of millions of people from starvation. It also created massive dependency on fertilizers, pesticides, and single-crop farming (monoculture), which damaged soil and water systems.

Today, agribusiness — large-scale corporate farming — dominates global food production. A handful of multinational companies control the majority of the world's seeds, fertilizers, and food supply chains. Supporters say this creates efficiency and feeds more people. Critics argue it pushes out small farmers, reduces crop diversity, and concentrates food power in too few hands.

GMOs are at the heart of this debate. Supporters say GMOs can create drought-resistant, nutrient-rich crops. Opponents worry about corporate control over seeds and biodiversity loss.

In Alberta, farming is part of the economy and culture. How globalization affects farmers here directly affects your community.

sessions.title: Session 3 — Pandemics, Technology, and the Limits of Globalization

elements.content: COVID-19 spread from Wuhan to Calgary in weeks. It then took the largest coordinated global scientific effort in history to fight it. The same globalization that spread the virus also built the tools to stop it.

elements.instruction: Type each definition.

elements.title: Kurzgesagt: The Coronavirus Explained & What You Should Do
elements.note: Kurzgesagt, March 2020, approximately 8.5 minutes, 88 million views. Fact-checked by virologists and epidemiologists. Explains how the virus spreads and the logic of global response. The 2020 date is a feature — students can compare what was predicted vs what actually happened.

chunks.title: How COVID spreads and why it matters globally

questions.text: COVID-19 is caused by a bacterium.
The virus spreads primarily through direct physical contact only.
The virus is especially dangerous because many people spread it while ___.
The video explains exponential spread. Using your own words and a simple example — why does exponential growth matter so much at the start of an outbreak?
This video was made in March 2020. Looking back now — what did it get right? What did we learn later that it didn't know yet?

chunks.title: Flattening the curve and global response

questions.text: Flattening the curve means fewer total cases overall.
The main goal of flattening the curve is to prevent ___ from being overwhelmed.
COVID became a global pandemic partly because of globalization — international travel, trade, and connected cities. Name TWO specific ways globalization accelerated the pandemic.
Name TWO ways globalization also helped fight the pandemic.

The video is now 5 years old. We know how it ended. Do you think the world was prepared for COVID? Was Canada? What would have made things better? Write 4-5 sentences using evidence from the video and what you know now.

elements.title: Technology: Connecting and Dividing
elements.content:
Globalization and technology are deeply linked. The internet, smartphones, and social media have both connected and divided us.

Connected: Global coordination on climate, pandemics, and human rights. Real-time communication across borders. Access to education and markets for billions. International youth movements organized overnight.

Divided: The digital divide — 26% internet access in low-income countries vs 95% in wealthy ones. Misinformation spreads at the same speed as facts. Social media contributing to youth mental health decline in wealthy countries. Surveillance and authoritarian control enabled by the same technology.

The same technology that let scientists share COVID vaccine data in 2020 also spread vaccine misinformation to millions. Technology doesn't have values — people do.

questions.text: Internet access is roughly equal across wealthy and poor countries.
what is the digital divide and why does it matter for globalization?
The context says 'technology doesn't have values — people do.' What does that mean? Do you agree?

elements.title: Global Pandemic Budget
elements.instruction: You are on a committee advising the Canadian government on pandemic preparedness. You have $10 million to allocate. The total must equal exactly $10M. Minimum $0 on any category.

options.label: Fund a global disease surveillance network
Support WHO and international health organizations

follow_up_questions.text: Explain your two biggest allocations — why did you prioritize those?
What does your allocation say about whether you see pandemic threats as primarily a national problem or a global one?

checkpoints.title: Civic Responsibilities in a Globalizing World

sessions.title: Session 2 — Responding to Globalization: From Buying to Governing

elements.content: 71% of Canadians said they planned to buy fewer American products in 2025. If that holds — the U.S. could lose $90 billion in revenue. A thought in someone's head becomes a purchase decision, which becomes a trend, which becomes an economic force. That's how power actually works.

elements.instruction: Type each definition.

elements.title: CNBC: Canadian Tourism Slump Costs U.S. Economy $4.5 Billion

chunks.title: Did the boycott actually work? The economic data

questions.text: Consumer boycotts always have immediate and measurable economic effects.
U.S. spirits exports to Canada fell measurably after the Canadian boycott.
The U.S. economy could lose up to $___billion in revenue due to international boycotts and declining tourism from Canada.
___ per cent of Canadians said they planned to buy fewer American products in 2025.
Experts say boycotts often 'wane over time' as people return to old habits. What would it take to make the Buy Canadian movement stick long-term? Write 3-4 sentences.
A buycott means deliberately buying from something you want to support. Name ONE company or type of product you would personally buycott to support a cause — and explain why.

Some economists argue boycotting American goods actually hurts Canadians — less competition means higher prices. Does the civic value outweigh the economic cost? Make your argument in 4-5 sentences using specific evidence from the video.

elements.title: Three Ways to Respond to Globalization
elements.content:
As an individual: Buy ethically (fair trade, local, sustainable). Boycott or buycott. Reduce your ecological footprint. Stay informed and vote. Speak up.

Through organizations: NGOs coordinate individual action into collective pressure. Labour unions protect workers across global supply chains. Student groups and youth climate movements amplify voices.

Through government: Trade agreements that include human rights standards. Foreign aid and development. Sanctions against human rights violators. Regulation of Canadian companies operating abroad.

questions.text: Which of the three levels do you think is most powerful for creating change? Why?
Can you think of a real example of each level working — individual action, organizational action, government action? They can be from anything in the course or from your own knowledge.
Are these three levels competing with each other or do they work together? Explain with a specific example.

sessions.title: Session 3 — Canada's Role in the World

elements.content: Canada doesn't have the world's largest military or economy. But Canada has a reputation — for peacekeeping, for multiculturalism, for multilateralism. Is that reputation earned? And does it come with obligations?

elements.instruction: Type each definition.

elements.title: What Canada Does on the World Stage
elements.content:
Canada is a member of the UN, G7, G20, NATO, NORAD, Commonwealth, La Francophonie, WTO, and dozens of other international organizations. We give foreign aid. We have peacekeepers. We have signed international agreements on human rights, climate, and trade.

Canada is also a major oil and gas producer, a significant arms exporter, home to many of the world's largest mining companies operating in developing countries, and a country that has repeatedly been cited by the UN for failing to uphold Indigenous rights at home.

Civic responsibility at the national level means asking: what should Canada be doing, and what is Canada actually doing? Are those the same thing?

questions.text: Name THREE international organizations Canada belongs to and briefly explain what each one does.
Canada's record on human rights is perfect both at home and abroad.
Canada is both an aid donor AND a country whose mining companies sometimes harm communities abroad. How do you make sense of both being true at the same time?

elements.title: Is Canada Living Up to Its Responsibilities?
elements.instruction: Rate Canada's performance in each area from 1 (failing badly) to 5 (doing very well). Then write a 4-5 sentence paragraph answering: Overall, is Canada a responsible global citizen?
items: Human rights at home including Indigenous rights
Labour standards in Canadian companies operating abroad

follow_up.text: Overall, is Canada a responsible global citizen? Write 4-5 sentences.

elements.title: Letter to Canada
elements.instruction: Write a short letter (5-7 sentences) to the Government of Canada about ONE specific thing you think Canada should do differently in its global civic responsibilities. Be specific — not just 'be better.' Name a concrete policy or action.

fields.label: Write your letter here.

elements.content: You're one person. The world's problems are enormous. Governments and international organizations have been trying to solve them for decades. So what exactly can YOU do? The answer might surprise you — but it also requires honesty about what doesn't work.

elements.instruction: Type each definition.

elements.title: Myth Busting — What Actually Works?
elements.instruction: Rate each belief about civic action from 1 (mostly a myth) to 5 (definitely true). Then explain your most controversial rating in 4-5 sentences with a real example.

items.text: Individual consumer choices can meaningfully change corporate behaviour.
Signing online petitions actually accomplishes something.
Voting is the most important civic act you can take.
Young people have as much political power as adults.
Social media posts create real-world change.
Boycotts hurt companies more than consumers.
One person cannot make a difference at the global level.
The most effective change happens slowly through institutions, not protests.

follow_up.text: Pick your most controversial rating and defend it in 4-5 sentences using a real example from this course or from your own knowledge.

elements.title: Your NGO Project
elements.content:
For your NGO project you will research one real organization that responds to a globalization-related issue. You will evaluate how effective it is.

Your NGO must: be real and currently active; connect to at least one issue from this course; work at the international or transnational level.

You will answer: What issue does it address? Who runs it? What does it actually do? What has it achieved? How does it fund itself? Is it effective — and how do you know?

Possible NGOs to consider (you can also find your own): Amnesty International, Oxfam, Doctors Without Borders, UNICEF, Greenpeace, Fair Trade Canada, Kimberley Process, Rainforest Alliance, Human Rights Watch, Free the Children / WE Charity, Engineers Without Borders, Plan International, Save the Children.
elements.teacher_note: Students research independently using their phones. Circulate and confirm NGOs are legitimate and appropriate.

elements.title: NGO Research
elements.instruction: Complete all fields using real research. You can use your phone. Be ready to show Mr. McRae your sources.
rows: One specific thing it has achieved with evidence
Connection to this course (name a checkpoint)

elements.title: NGO Evaluation
elements.instruction: Write your evaluations in full sentences. These are your own opinions — not just facts from the website.

fields.label: What is this NGO trying to do and does it have a clear theory of change — what does it think will happen if it does X?
How does this NGO connect to what you learned in this course? Name a specific checkpoint.
What is the strongest argument that this NGO is effective?
What is the most honest criticism of this NGO?
Would you donate money or volunteer time to this NGO? Why or why not?
Overall — is this NGO part of the solution to globalization's problems, or is it more of a band-aid? Write 4-5 sentences making your case.

elements.title: NGO One-Pager
elements.instruction: Design a simple one-pager for your NGO as if you are trying to recruit a classmate to volunteer or donate. It does not need to be beautiful — it needs to be honest and specific.
elements.teacher_note: Students can create this digitally in the app or on paper. Physical version should be photographed and uploaded if possible.
required_elements: NGO name and a simple logo idea
The problem the NGO addresses in one sentence
What the NGO does — 3 specific bullet points
One thing it has actually achieved
Why someone YOUR AGE should care
One way a student could help right now

follow_up.text: What was the hardest part of making this honest? Write 2-3 sentences.

elements.title: Course Journey Tracker
elements.instruction: Look back across all nine checkpoints. Write the most important thing you learned in each one — just a few words or a sentence.
columns: The most important thing I learned

elements.title: Final Position Statement
elements.instruction: You have now completed this course. Answer these questions in full sentences. This is your final position — make it honest.

fields.label: What is globalization? Answer in 2-3 sentences as if explaining to someone who has never heard the word.
Is globalization more good or bad overall? Take a clear position and defend it in 4-5 sentences using at least THREE specific examples from this course.
Who benefits most from globalization? Name specific groups and explain why.
Who is most harmed? Name specific groups and explain why.
Should I, as a citizen, respond to globalization? Write 4-5 sentences giving your honest final position.
What will you actually do? Name ONE concrete action — not an essay, not 'raise awareness.' A real specific thing. And explain why you chose it.

elements.title: Big Checkpoint 4 — Bring Everything
elements.instruction: Flag Mr. McRae when you are ready. Bring your NGO research notes and your final position statement.

Your NGO — what it does, whether it works, why you chose it
One course experience that genuinely changed how you see the world
One specific thing you will do as a result of this course
Your answer to: Should I, as a citizen, respond to globalization?
